item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, I upvoted but something bothered me:

surrounded at all possible access points by signs in English and French warning of falling rocks

Why the English signs? When I read that I assumed the location would be in a bilingual area, maybe in some areas of Canada for example but no, France. Is the Foundation really so Anglocentric that it insists on using English? Even though, if anything, that's going to increase interest in the site?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have said I don't like the expungements. But liked enough to upvote anyways.

For me, the problem with the way it handles paradox is either it says something so bad that they need to expunge it or something beyond speech happens. There's a huge difference between, say, it starts saying foundation secrets and it starts popping heads. I feel like expungement should imply which of the two it is.

I would like it a lot more if either it specifically said "SCP-1044: [DATA EXPUNGED]" or implied something more drastic happened, maybe saying medical personnel should be onsite when testing after incident x.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only way this makes sense to me is if the echo "decides" the contradiction based on the opinions of listeners.

Both A and F start without having given it much thought, and they both on some level automatically assume the opposite of "hello" is "goodbye". It's not strictly logical, but they don't think about that until afterwards.
But Dr. C has pondered this beforehand, and specifically chose a word that he didn't think had a logical contradiction. So the echo delivered on his pre-assessment.
If my theory is correct, then if F has been alone and shouted "dog" he would have got back "cat"

This would also potentially shine some limited light on what happens with paradoxes. Everyone present for that test would presumably have expected something drastic to happen, so that's what the echo did.

I'm picturing the infinite logic loop manifesting as an infinite number of "this statement is false" and "this statement is true" responses delivered simultaneously. Only the physical limitations of air pressure kept it from being infinitely loud. Even so, it would be a four second pressure wave to beat the largest bombs ever made.

Let's not do that again, indeed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it, but that EXPUNGED hits you like a rock. It's so sudden and it appears right when your interest is spiking.
I understand why it's there, but instead of a full EXPUNGED it could be the experiment carried but the results are what is censored.
As in "A says "potato"
"1044 then goes on to [DATA EXPUNGED]"
"Testing is now strictly controled and a procedure is now in development to avoid 1044 from
###### again."
Something that leaves it to the imagination but that isn't an abrupt cut to the reader.
I feel this could work well here. Nonetheless, it's a pretty neat safe scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is nice, but I didn't like the expungement. I'd prefer if there wasn't an entire log that was just expunged without any hint of what was there; it works in 610, but it already has a lot of excellent explorations before it, giving a little hint of what's in the last log… redacting the paradox worked tough.
No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I love that idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. I really like this one.

…I dislike it. I don't really like this one.

(by the way, it's supposed my be my echo, in case people don't get it and take it the wrong way)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ummmm…true, I'll go true. Thought to be honest, I might have heard that one before. Kind of cheating…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to imagine that the third test resulted in an avalanche that crushed the researchers and the echo saying, "Think you're so damn clever, huh?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OH god, that's hilarious. imagine if someone made a video of that. an SCP video series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, though one problem I have with it is that I believe that 'what it is' should always be in the beginning of the description. Not in the second last paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. C████: I suppose there is no opposite statement to dog.

Lies, it is banshees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Its quite obvious that saying, "This statement is false" will result in a tear in the fabric of reality, including, but not limited to, a tesseract, black hole, quantum singularity, or worm hole…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The best part of that scene is that all the little lobotomized box robots all exploded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh wait, I know this one, don't tell me, um, I know this one!
Um…
Yes, I'm going to go with yes. That seems like the correct answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Researcher F██████: [shouting] This sentence is false.

[SCP-1044 does not respond]

[Researcher F██████'s head suddenly explodes]

No more paradoxes! -Dr. C████

I'm sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THIS……SENTENCE……IS…….FALSE!
(Don't think about it, don't think about it, don't think about it……)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was going to upvote, but then

Experiment 1044-3

[DATA EXPUNGED]

In the later experiments, attempts were made to test SCP-1044 by calling out self-contradictory statements such as 'This sentence is false.' This invariably resulted in [DATA EXPUNGED]. Future experiments are to avoid such attempts.

What? Does it just scream at you? '~' -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought the same thing <3

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured the response to "This statement is false!" would have been "No it's not!".

Upvoted for elegance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Avalanches involve the falling of snow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This statement bothers me…

SCP-1044 is to be surrounded at all possible access points by signs in English and French warning of falling rocks, avalanches, landmines and dangerous animals.

Landmines? And are not falling rocks and avalanches the same thing?

and I would figure the statement

This sentence is false

Would simply result in it echoing "This sentence is not false"

anyway, enjoyed and upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It replies with random gibberish in German.
"Bonjour!"
"Auf Wiedersehen!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't help but think that the [DATA EXPUNGED] is in there to encourage Foundation personnel to take an otherwise more trivial SCP more seriously (and nothing of special note really happens).

And, I like this. It's one of the SCPs that most obviously is completely harmless, but is being contained by the Foundation purely to preserve normalcy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. Count me in on the "I hated the redaction but up voted anyway" train

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, I didn't know that existed, but I love that it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's exactly what I was thinking.
What if someone who doesn't speak french shouts random gibberish in french?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda reminds me of the Candid Camera prank with the spanish-speaking echo.

"Spanish!" "Ingles! -es -es"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, though I wonder if there might be some sort of happy compromise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's just fine as-is. I mean, trying to fake it out having some sort of horrible result? That's just great, it doesn't matter what the result is. Knowing that knowing the result is not for the casual observer is enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll have a look at it. See how that end might be rejigged slightly while keeping in the same spirit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Resulted in [DATA EXPUNGED]" is just typically a bad thing. There should be at least a part of the result visible or it just looks lazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Exactly. The opposite of 1 is -1, not 0. Thus, the opposite of dog would be "negative dog", for which there is no real word.

That said, it's been a while since I saw a nice, simple, wholesomely safe entry that WORKED. Congrats. Also I like to imagine the paradoxical thing simply being "This statement is true", but that was too simple and the researchers spiced it up to make it sound better (compare the infamous "$10,000 toilet seat" in the Space Shuttle)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any particular one? It's possible I can remove the first one and replace it with something, but the other two… the thing is, I have an idea (or about three slightly different ideas) about what has been expunged, but I think writing up any of them would be less good than just leaving that information out and letting some imaginations go at it.

I may add additional logs. One of the ones I was thinking of adding was a chronological detail of everything the echo has been recorded as saying. Another one was an extension of the second experiment where the Dr. and the Researcher talk some more about whether the echo is logically consistent or not. But I do think the information that is left out of this one is one of the things that makes it a little creepy, and not just kind of silly and amusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The [DATA EXPUNGED] really bothers me, but I like it. Upvote, but please take care of the expunged. ;~;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. Simple and creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the later experiments, attempts were made to test SCP-1044 by calling out self-contradictory statements such as 'This sentence is false.' This invariably resulted in [DATA EXPUNGED]. Future experiments are to avoid such attempts.

ugh

upvote though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking that is a good instinct to follow, too. It definitely worked here.

But I do like your idea of adding logs later— maybe in the vein of SCP-914 or SCP-294, with a mix of cracky and unsettling outcomes. I think keeping death and entrails out of it would be for the better, by far, but it might be creepy to have someone just… vanish. Or alterations of speech/perception, in the same logic-vein.

Even without any of that, a bit more lead up to the [NOPE DENIED] might make the hammer drop a bit heavier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1044 = ghost of a pissy Aristotle

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Straightforward, interesting, and kind of funny. You have my upvote, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah! If it helps, it was hard to do the expunging. I really wanted to talk about it but I had a strong feeling it was better not to. Still, I might append supplementary logs at a later date if I think there's a good case for having some more echo demonstrations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like to think that the echo just said "Oh, fuck you" in test 3. Or maybe responded with "This statement is just as false"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple. Logic humour. I like it— I just started guessing at potential horrors early on, and was a little let down by how MUCH was [EXPUNGED]. Still, less is generally better than too much … upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Out of all the expungements, this is the one I want to actually read the most. Go fig!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps so! But I'm not sure. I'd say that's not the polar opposite, it's just the lack of a thing. The opposite of a dog would be some sort of un-dog, and even then it's unclear whether than would be a logically opposite statement.

When I was writing this I realised there are lots of ways to get oneself into trouble and tied in knots when dealing with opposites, logic and language. So I decided that instead of trying to solve the problem head-on, I wanted the logs to hint at a certain amount of uncertainty over the fallibility and (by implication) sentience of the echo. I'm hoping it pays off :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't the opposite of "dog" be "absence of dog" ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. Simplicity works. Not knowing what happened with the paradoxes was a wise choice, I think. Avoids complicating it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent. One of the oldschool , plain weird articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This guy gets it. :'D

I wonder what happened with the paradoxical echoes.. hmm..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unusual, and weird. I agree with Dexanote, this is a nice simple SCP, though i'd love to see more echoes, but that might be just me wanting more. Upvoted, nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple, straightforward, a hint of humour, and truly Safe. This is good. I like this a lot.
